common_name: Musquée de Provence Pumpkin (French Heirloom Large Flat Ribbed Pumpkin, Superior Flavor)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; Musquée de Provence requires a long growing season (95–110 days) and produces very large vines (10–15 feet) requiring substantial space. In zone 9, plant March–April and allow the vine to run — give each plant 100+ sq ft of growing space. The fruit matures over a tan-to-orange skin progression: harvest when the entire surface has turned from green-gray to tan-brown with deeply ribbed grooves, typically by October. The large flat form (15–35 lb, 12–18 inch diameter, 6–10 inches tall) is unmistakable. In zone 9 the long growing season fits well, allowing harvest before frost in a climate where the long warm period suits the slow maturation of this large-fruited type.

directions: Direct sow 2–3 seeds in hills 5–6 feet apart; thin to one plant per hill. Allow full vine development. Harvest when skin is uniformly tan-brown. Musquée de Provence is one of the great heirloom pumpkins of French cuisine — a Cucurbita maxima variety from Provence in southern France that has been cultivated for centuries and remains one of the most prized cooking pumpkins in French markets. The fruits are dramatically distinctive in form: very large (15–35 lbs), extremely flat and disc-shaped with deep, pronounced ribs running from stem to blossom end, and a skin that progresses from gray-green in the growing season to the characteristic tan-brown to warm russet color at full maturity. This tan-ribbed mature appearance is what makes Musquée de Provence so visually striking in fall garden displays and farmers markets — the deep ribbing creates dramatic light and shadow effects unlike any other pumpkin form. The culinary properties are outstanding: the dense, bright orange flesh is sweet, complex, and richly flavored with a buttery, slightly nutty quality that has made this variety the preferred pumpkin for soupe de potiron (pumpkin soup), gratins, and purees in Provençal cooking. The flesh holds its texture and flavor through cooking better than many pumpkin types, and the high dry-matter content makes Musquée de Provence flesh particularly useful in baking and thick purées where excess moisture in standard pumpkin types is a problem. The seeds are edible and have a pleasant mild flavor when roasted.
sources: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Musquée de Provence pumpkin French heirloom variety notes
Territorial Seed Company — Musquée de Provence pumpkin variety description and trial data
Les Jardins de l'Écoumène — Traditional French pumpkin and squash variety documentation

common_name: Boothby's Blonde Cucumber (Maine Heirloom Cream-Yellow Oval Cucumber, Seed Savers Classic)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Boothby's Blonde is a semi-vining variety (grows 3–4 feet before trailing) that can be trained up a trellis for cleaner fruit or allowed to sprawl. In zone 9, plant March–May and again August–September for fall production. Harvest fruits when cream-yellow in color (3–4 inches long) — do not allow to become large and yellow-orange, which indicates overripeness, bitterness, and seed hardening. The pale skin of Boothby's Blonde makes it easy to spot overripe fruits before they develop bitterness, unlike dark-green varieties where overripeness is harder to detect. The black spines (as opposed to the white spines of burpless/European types) indicate this is a traditional pickling-style heirloom, though it is most often eaten fresh.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost; thin to 12–18 inches. Harvest at 3–4 inches when cream-yellow and firm. Boothby's Blonde is a Maine family heirloom cucumber preserved by the Boothby family from Livermore Falls, Maine, and shared through the Seed Savers Exchange — the network through which most American home-saved heirloom vegetable varieties have been maintained and distributed since the organization's founding in 1975. The cucumber is immediately distinguishable from every other commonly grown variety by its fruit color: the skin is creamy white to pale yellow at harvest maturity, oval to oblong in shape (3–4 inches long), with black spines on the surface ribs rather than the white spines of European or Asian cucumber types. This combination of cream-yellow skin and black spines produces a visual character unlike any other cucumber commonly available — the pale fruit with dark spines stands out dramatically in the garden and on the plate. The eating quality is excellent: thin skin (no peeling needed), crisp and juicy flesh, mild sweet flavor with no bitterness, and a good seed cavity for a traditional cucumber type. Boothby's Blonde exemplifies the category of heirloom vegetables that persist not because of exceptional yield or disease resistance but because of unique visual and flavor qualities unavailable in commercial varieties — preserved specifically because gardeners valued those qualities enough to maintain the seed across generations.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Boothby's Blonde cucumber Maine heirloom documentation
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Boothby's Blonde cucumber variety notes
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Heritage cucumber variety descriptions

common_name: Salt and Pepper Cucumber (AAS-Winning White Speckled Seedless Cucumber)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Salt and Pepper is a parthenocarpic (sets fruit without pollination) compact vining cucumber that can be grown in tunnels or outdoors. In zone 9, plant March–May for spring production and August for fall. The parthenocarpic character means the plant produces fruit without bees visiting — an advantage in early spring or in protected tunnel growing environments where pollinator access is limited. Harvest at 3–4 inches before the characteristic speckled pattern begins to fade to solid cream — once the speckles disappear the cucumber has become slightly overripe and may develop a slightly bitter note. The compact vine (4–5 feet) does not require extensive trellising but benefits from support for cleaner, straighter fruit.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost; thin to 12 inches. Harvest at 3–4 inches when speckles are visible. Salt and Pepper is an F1 hybrid cucumber that received All-America Selections recognition for its distinctive appearance and exceptional eating quality. The fruit skin is white to pale cream with characteristic dark green to near-black speckles distributed across the surface — the 'salt and pepper' pattern that gives the variety its name and makes it immediately visually recognizable. Unlike heirloom types with similar light-colored skin (such as Boothby's Blonde), Salt and Pepper is an F1 hybrid bred for the parthenocarpic (seedless) character that allows fruit set without pollination — the interior is nearly seed-free and extremely tender, with no tough seed cavity. The resulting texture is among the finest of any outdoor cucumber: exceptionally crisp, with virtually no bitterness, tender skin that requires no peeling, and very high water content producing a refreshing fresh-eating quality. Salt and Pepper produces abundantly and early — with the first ripe fruit typically appearing 57 days from direct sowing, making it one of the earliest-producing cucumbers available. For zone 9 gardeners, the early maturity and heat-set ability allow the variety to produce heavily in spring before summer heat becomes limiting, and the fall planting typically produces prolifically through October or November. The seedless character and very thin skin make Salt and Pepper one of the few cucumbers that market gardeners can sell without refrigeration for brief periods, as the intact skin and low seed cavity reduce the moisture loss that causes conventional cucumbers to shrivel.
sources: All-America Selections — Salt and Pepper cucumber AAS winner recognition
Harris Seeds — Salt and Pepper cucumber commercial production notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Parthenocarpic cucumber variety comparison

common_name: Magda Squash (Lebanese Cousa-Type Summer Squash, Superior Nutty-Sweet Flavor)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Magda is a bush-type squash (no trailing vines) that produces compact, upright plants suitable for small gardens and raised beds. In zone 9, plant March–May for spring production and August for fall. Harvest Magda squash at 3–4 inches for best flavor — the nutty, sweet quality that distinguishes cousa-type squash from standard zucchini is most pronounced at this young harvest stage. Slightly larger fruits (4–6 inches) are good for stuffing; overripe fruits become bland and seedy. The pale, almost celery-green skin of Magda is a useful visual cue for harvest maturity — harvest when skin is pale and slightly waxy. In hot Gulf Coast summers, squash plants typically decline after 6–8 weeks; replanting in August extends the season through fall.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost; thin to 24–36 inches. Harvest at 3–4 inches for best flavor; up to 6 inches for stuffing. Magda is a cousa-type Lebanese summer squash — a form of summer squash with a fundamentally different eating character from the Italian zucchini types (such as Black Beauty, Costata Romanesco) that dominate American garden squash production. The cousa squash group, originating in the Middle East and Mediterranean, produces fruits that are pale sage-green to celery-green, cylindrical to club-shaped with a distinctively bulging blossom end, and with a pale, waxy skin surface that is noticeably different in appearance and texture from the darker, shinier skin of Italian zucchini types. The defining characteristic of cousa squash flavor — and the reason Magda has a devoted following among cooks — is a nutty, creamy sweetness distinctly different from the mild, slightly vegetal flavor of standard zucchini. Cousa squash flesh has a firmer, drier texture and more pronounced sweetness than Italian types, making it the preferred squash for Middle Eastern preparations: the primary culinary use of cousa squash in Lebanese, Syrian, and Egyptian cooking is kousa mahshi (literally 'stuffed squash') — the small fruits cored and filled with a spiced meat and rice mixture before being simmered in tomato sauce, a preparation where the firmer flesh and nutty flavor of the cousa type are essential to the dish's character. Magda is an F1 hybrid cousa-type that produces prolifically with good uniformity, adapts well to North American growing conditions, and provides the cousa flavor character more reliably than open-pollinated Middle Eastern varieties.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Magda cousa squash variety notes and culinary description
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Cousa squash variety background
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Summer Squash Variety Selection Guide

common_name: Henderson Lima Bean (Baby Bush Lima, Heat-Tolerant Gulf South Butter Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil (65°F minimum); Henderson is a baby lima (smaller seed than Fordhook types) that excels in hot weather — it sets pods reliably in the Gulf South heat that causes many bean varieties to drop flowers. In zone 9, plant April through July for summer production; late plantings mature in September when temperatures moderate for the fall harvest. The bush habit (no staking needed) and compact plant size (18–24 inches) make Henderson practical for raised beds and small gardens. Plant at 4-inch spacing in rows 24 inches apart for the highest yield per square foot. Henderson is widely available as a named 'baby butter bean' in Gulf South seed racks and is one of the most heat-adapted dry and fresh lima bean types for the region.

directions: Direct sow 1–1.5 inches deep in warm soil; thin to 4–6 inches. Harvest green for fresh use when pods are plump; leave to dry on the plant for dry beans. Henderson is a small-seeded bush lima bean — one of the most widely grown lima bean varieties in the American South and Gulf Coast region, where it is often marketed and sold as 'baby butter bean' or 'speckled butter bean' (referring to the speckled seed coloring of some strains). Henderson was introduced in the 1880s and has remained a staple in Southern gardening for over a century, valued for its small, tender seeds, heat tolerance, and reliable production in the warm, humid conditions of the Gulf South that challenge larger-seeded lima types (such as Fordhook). The primary distinction between Henderson and Fordhook-type limas is seed size: Henderson produces small seeds roughly half the size of Fordhook, with a more tender skin and a creamier texture when cooked fresh or dried. In the Gulf South culinary tradition, 'butter beans' (the Southern name for fresh lima beans) are a staple of summer cooking — simmered low and slow with fatback or ham hock until very tender, the small Henderson seeds achieve a creamy, buttery texture that the variety's Southern common name accurately describes. Henderson also performs well as a dry bean: the small seeds dry quickly on the vine and cook to a smooth, creamy texture in soups and stews without requiring the long soaking and cooking time of larger bean types. The heat tolerance of Henderson is a practical advantage in zone 9: while cool-season beans (snap beans, peas) cannot be grown in summer, Henderson sets pods reliably in July-August temperatures that stop production in more sensitive varieties.
sources: Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Henderson lima bean heritage butter bean documentation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Lima Bean Production for the Gulf South
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Warm-Season Bean and Pea Variety Recommendations
